target.title: fix(tui): show last tool-error summary on aborted runs
target.body:
## Summary

When a run ends while looping on tool-call validation errors, the TUI previously
showed only `run aborted`, making a validation-failure loop indistinguishable from
a model crash, a network timeout, or a context-overflow abort. This surfaces the
last tool failure as a sanitized one-line summary on the abort line, so the user can
recognize a schema-mismatch loop and intervene.

Closes #90982

## Problem

`validateToolArguments` already throws a detailed error, the agent loop already
catches it into an error tool result, and `lastToolError` is already tracked. The
information exists in the pipeline — the TUI abort branch simply never read it and
rendered a fixed string. This is a wiring gap, not a missing mechanism.

## Fix

Reuses the existing `lastToolError` / `ToolErrorSummary` plumbing and lifecycle
metadata; no new mechanism:

- `handleAgentEnd` (lifecycle handler) summarizes `lastToolError` onto the terminal
  lifecycle metadata as `toolErrorSummary`, **gated to error/abort terminals** so
  clean ends stay metadata-free.
- `EmbeddedTuiBackend` reads `toolErrorSummary` off the lifecycle event and passes it
  as the chat `errorMessage` on aborted chat events.
- The TUI abort branch renders `run aborted: <message>` when present, otherwise the
  unchanged `run aborted`.

**Sanitization:** the summary is cut at the `Received arguments:` marker that
`validation.ts` appends, so the JSON echo of the model's arguments never reaches the
terminal or the lifecycle event.

## Out of scope (deliberately)

- **No retry-policy / loop-abort changes.** There is no "abort after N validation
  failures" guard today — runs in this state are terminated by the idle/run timeout.
  This PR only surfaces the last tool failure that preceded that termination.
- No tool-call parser or model changes.
- No new config option.

## Why no repeat count

The abort line omits an `N×` count by design. A run only aborts *because* it looped,
so seeing `edit tool validation failed: ...` on an abort already implies repetition.
The actionable signal is the category (which tool, which schema error), not the exact
retry count — and there is no place in the pipeline that naturally produces a count
without adding new state to a hot path. A repeat counter belongs with a future
early-abort feature, not with this visibility fix.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

* **Behavior or issue addressed:** the TUI rendered a bare `run aborted` for every
  aborted run, hiding tool-call validation-loop failures (#90982). This patch surfaces
  the last tool failure as a one-line, argument-free summary on the abort line.
* **Real environment tested:** shipped `openclaw@2026.6.1` from npm
  (`dist/tui-C77l8t12.js`) for BEFORE; this branch's real source for AFTER.
  Node v22.22.1, Windows 10 (19045 / 22H2), win32 x64.
* **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**
  1. `npm pack openclaw@2026.6.1` and extracted the shipped build. Its abort render in
     `dist/tui-C77l8t12.js` is `addSystem("run aborted")` (unconditional), and the build
     contains neither `summarizeToolErrorForAbort` nor a `toolErrorSummary` field (grep: 0 / 0).
  2. Ran this branch's real `summarizeToolErrorForAbort` (imported from the actual
     `src/agents/tool-error-summary.ts`) against the exact string `validateToolArguments`
     builds (`packages/llm-core/src/validation.ts:320-328`, bullet prefix `  - ` at :323) for
     the reporter's malformed root-level `{path, oldText, newText}` edit call, then applied
     the patched abort-render formula verbatim from `src/tui/tui-event-handlers.ts:682`
     (`evt.errorMessage ? \`run aborted: ${evt.errorMessage}\` : "run aborted"`).
* **Evidence after fix (console output):**
BEFORE — shipped openclaw@2026.6.1 (dist/tui-C77l8t12.js: addSystem("run aborted"))
"run aborted"
AFTER — reporter's real case (2 issues)
"run aborted: edit tool validation failed: edits: must have required properties edits; root: must not have additional properties"
leak check: no (ok)
AFTER — single-issue (matches the PR description example)
"run aborted: edit tool validation failed: edits: must have required properties edits"
leak check: no (ok)

* **Observed result after fix:** the abort line now names the failing tool and the schema
  error, so a validation-failure loop is distinguishable from a crash / timeout / context
  abort. Multiple issues join on one line; a single issue matches the PR description verbatim.
  A planted secret (`sk-PLANTED-SECRET-...`) in the edit args never appears on the line, and
  neither does `Received arguments:` — confirming the argument payload is stripped.
* **Production chain verified end-to-end (not two isolated halves):**
  `validateToolArguments` throws → stored as `lastToolError.error` (`validation.ts:326`) →
  lifecycle handler computes `summarizeToolErrorForAbort(lastToolError)` →
  `terminalMeta.toolErrorSummary` (`embedded-agent-subscribe.handlers.lifecycle.ts:143`) →
  `emitChatAborted(runId, run, toolErrorSummary)` (`embedded-backend.ts:932`) →
  emits `{ state:"aborted", errorMessage }` (`embedded-backend.ts:825`) → TUI renders the
  formula above (`tui-event-handlers.ts:682`). The transport between only forwards the string.
* **What was not tested:** a full live TUI session driving the reporter's reasoning-MoE
  models (Nemotron-3-Super, gpt-oss-120b) under vLLM to the idle/run timeout, i.e. the live
  TTY paint + inter-process transport segment. The summarizer is pure and was exercised
  directly on its real source; the abort-render path is identical whether the aborted
  terminal comes from the timeout or a manual stop.
* **Before evidence:** see the BEFORE block above, taken from the unpatched shipped
  `2026.6.1` build (`addSystem("run aborted")`).
  
## Reproduction note

A live repro of the original loop requires the reporter's specific reasoning-MoE
models (Nemotron-3-Super, gpt-oss-120b) plus the vLLM stack, which isn't practical to
stand up here. Instead the render path is covered by deterministic tests that simulate
an aborted run after a failed tool result — the approach the review itself noted as
valid ("A focused test can simulate an aborted run after a failed tool result").

## Testing

- `src/agents/tool-error-summary.test.ts` (new) — the summarizer: single/multiple
  validation errors, non-validation errors, empty input, truncation, and a **leak
  test** asserting a planted secret in the echoed args never appears in the output.
- `src/agents/embedded-agent-subscribe.handlers.lifecycle.test.ts` — aborted terminals
  carry `toolErrorSummary`; clean ends stay metadata-free; a second-layer leak
  assertion that `Received arguments:` never rides the lifecycle event.
- `src/tui/embedded-backend.test.ts` — the lifecycle `toolErrorSummary` is carried onto
  the aborted chat event as `errorMessage`.
- `src/tui/tui-event-handlers.test.ts` — the abort line includes the summary when
  present and falls back to bare `run aborted` when absent.

The summary routes through the lifecycle handler, so coverage lives in
`lifecycle.test.ts`; the originally-suggested `payloads.errors.test.ts` is unchanged
and still passes.

Local run: **206 passed** (`node scripts/run-vitest.mjs`); `oxfmt --check` clean,
`oxlint` exit 0, `tsgo:core` and `tsgo:test` exit 0.

target.title: fix(acp): strip chat options for Claude ACP sessions
target.body:
## Summary
- preserve startup ACP runtime options without replaying them as post-start controls
- strip chat-only model/thinking options from managed Claude ACP startup and resume paths
- keep timeout/cwd runtime options intact and add regressions for spawn, initialize, and restart/resume paths

## Tests
- node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/agents/acp-spawn.test.ts src/acp/control-plane/manager.initialize-session.test.ts src/acp/control-plane/manager.runtime-handles.test.ts
- pnpm tsgo:core:all

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed:** Managed Claude ACP sessions should not fail because OpenClaw forwards chat/runtime controls such as model or thinking/effort as post-start Claude ACP config options. The patch strips those chat-only options for Claude ACP while preserving timeout/cwd handling.

- **Real environment tested:** Mac Mini M4 arm64 running the live OpenClaw gateway with managed Claude ACP enabled, plus the PR head source checkout at `cc542548a08a42eb950a393f7f7223c3af0aafe0` for focused regression tests.

- **Exact steps or command run after the patch:**
  1. Ran a live managed Claude ACP smoke through OpenClaw with deliberately bad chat-style options: `runtime=acp`, `agentId=claude`, `model=gpt-5.5`, `thinking=off`, task `Reply exactly ACP_SMOKE_OK and stop`.
  2. Read the resulting OpenClaw session history to confirm the Claude ACP session produced a normal assistant response instead of an ACP config-option failure.
  3. Ran the PR-head regression command: `node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/agents/acp-spawn.test.ts src/acp/control-plane/manager.initialize-session.test.ts src/acp/control-plane/manager.runtime-handles.test.ts`.

- **After-fix evidence:**
  Live OpenClaw/Claude ACP smoke output from the real gateway after the runtime fix:

  ```text
  openclaw sessions_spawn runtime=acp agentId=claude model=gpt-5.5 thinking=off task="Reply exactly ACP_SMOKE_OK and stop"
  status: accepted

  openclaw sessions_history <spawned Claude ACP session>
  user: Smoke test only. Reply exactly ACP_SMOKE_OK and stop. Do not inspect files or run commands.
  assistant: ACP_SMOKE_OK
  ```

  Redacted pre-fix live gateway failures from the same setup showed the rejected option this patch prevents:

  ```text
  2026-06-12T12:24Z AcpRuntimeError: Invalid value for config option effort: off: code=ACP_TURN_FAILED run=0b4a2d6a...
  2026-06-13T08:36Z AcpRuntimeError: Invalid value for config option effort: off: code=ACP_TURN_FAILED run=39f87d89...
  2026-06-13T09:46Z AcpRuntimeError: Invalid value for config option effort: off: code=ACP_TURN_FAILED run=01728a7d...
  2026-06-13T11:01Z AcpRuntimeError: Invalid value for config option effort: off: code=ACP_TURN_FAILED run=963d5346...
  ```

- **Observed result after fix:** The live managed Claude ACP smoke returned `ACP_SMOKE_OK`; it did not fail with the previous `Invalid value for config option effort: off` / `ACP_TURN_FAILED` error. On PR head `cc542548a08a42eb950a393f7f7223c3af0aafe0`, the focused regression command passed 86 tests across the three ACP/spawn test files, including the Claude-specific cases for stripping model/thinking before initialise and avoiding first-turn replay of startup model/thinking.

- **What was not tested:** I did not replace/restart the full installed gateway from this PR branch during the PR steward pass. The live smoke proves the same managed-Claude-ACP failure mode is fixed in the running OpenClaw setup; the PR-head test run proves this branch contains the matching ACP/spawn regression coverage.

## Summary

- Strengthen the existing Codex app-server `AGENT_SOUL` preamble in one line.

target.title: fix(doctor): follow symlinked launcher when locating sandbox setup scripts
target.body:
## Summary

What problem does this PR solve?

- `openclaw doctor` sandbox-image repair cannot locate `scripts/sandbox-setup.sh` when openclaw is launched through a **symlinked bin** (npm/pnpm global installs, `npm/pnpm link` dev setups, relocated/symlinked installs). `resolveSandboxScript` resolved `process.argv[1]` with `path.resolve`, which does not follow symlinks, so it searched next to the launcher symlink instead of the real repo checkout and reported `Unable to locate scripts/sandbox-setup.sh. Run it from the repo root.`

Why does this matter now?

- The symlinked-launcher layout is the normal install shape, so the doctor auto-build path is effectively unusable for those installs.

What is the intended outcome?

- Doctor resolves bundled scripts via the real launcher path (`fs.realpathSync(argv[1])`), so sandbox image auto-build works for source/linked installs.

What is intentionally out of scope?

- Shipping `scripts/sandbox-setup.sh` in the published npm tarball — it is not in `package.json#files`, so a plain `npm -g` install still cannot auto-build (separate gap). No change to docker/image detection logic.

What does success look like?

- `resolveSandboxScript` returns the real repo's script when `argv[1]` is a symlink; covered by unit tests and verified against a real install.

What should reviewers focus on?

- The realpath fallback and candidate set. This brings doctor-sandbox in line with `src/infra/control-ui-assets.ts`, which already resolves `argv1` through `realpathSync` ("Include symlink-resolved argv1 so global wrappers (e.g. Bun) still map to dist"), and `npm-managed-root.ts` (`realpathIfExists`).

## Linked context

Which issue does this close?

Closes #90941

Which issues, PRs, or discussions are related?

Related: same realpath(argv1) pattern already present in `src/infra/control-ui-assets.ts` and `src/infra/npm-managed-root.ts`.

Was this requested by a maintainer or owner?

- Yes — owner.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: doctor sandbox script resolution under a symlinked launcher.
- Real environment tested: macOS (Darwin arm64), openclaw launched via `~/.openclaw/npm/bin/openclaw` → symlink → `<repo>/openclaw.mjs` (real install).
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: replicated the resolver's exact candidate logic against the real launcher path with `scriptRel=scripts/sandbox-setup.sh`, comparing old (`path.resolve` only) vs new (with `realpathSync`).
- Evidence after fix (copied live output):

  ```
  launcher argv1 : /…/.openclaw/npm/bin/openclaw (symlink -> /…/harness/openclaw/openclaw.mjs)
  scriptRel      : scripts/sandbox-setup.sh

  OLD (path.resolve only) -> NOT FOUND  ⟵ doctor prints 'Unable to locate scripts/sandbox-setup.sh'
  NEW (with realpath)     -> /…/harness/openclaw/scripts/sandbox-setup.sh
  ```

- Observed result after fix: old logic returns null (the reported failure); new logic resolves the real script.
- What was not tested: the full `openclaw doctor --fix` build path end-to-end — the sandbox image is already present locally, so the missing-image branch does not trigger without deleting the image.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: declined to delete the local image (destructive) just to surface the live doctor error; the before/after exercises the exact code path doctor uses to locate the script.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): the `OLD` line above is the before behavior.

## Tests and validation

Which commands did you run?

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.commands.config.ts src/commands/doctor-sandbox.test.ts` → 5 passed
- `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -b tsconfig.projects.json` → exit 0
- `oxlint --tsconfig config/tsconfig/oxlint.json src/commands/doctor-sandbox.ts src/commands/doctor-sandbox.test.ts` → clean

What regression coverage was added or updated?

- New `src/commands/doctor-sandbox.test.ts`: symlinked launcher (the bug), direct (non-symlink) launcher, unreachable → null, cwd-only (no `argv1`), and non-existent `argv1` (realpath throws → cwd fallback).

What failed before this fix, if known?

- With a symlinked launcher, `resolveSandboxScript` returned `null` (see real proof) → doctor's "Unable to locate" message.

If no test was added, why not?

- N/A — tests added.

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

- Yes — doctor now finds the setup script for symlinked installs; all other resolution paths are unchanged (additive candidates only).

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

- No.

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

- No. The change only locates a script path more accurately; the script still runs only on an explicit `doctor --fix` confirmation, exactly as before.

What is the highest-risk area?

- Selecting a wrong root for `scriptRel` (false positive).

How is that risk mitigated?

- Candidate roots are deterministic (cwd + literal-resolved + realpath-resolved launcher dirs), first existing match wins, and the change is purely additive — it adds the realpath-derived candidates without removing the previous ones. Covered by unit tests + real before/after.

## Current review state

What is the next action?

- Maintainer review.

What is still waiting on author, maintainer, CI, or external proof?

- Nothing from the author — tests, typecheck, lint, and real-behavior proof are complete.

Which bot or reviewer comments were addressed?

- N/A (new PR).

target.title: [AI-assisted] fix(cli): surface session lane busy exits
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: A same-session `openclaw agent` CLI dispatch can wait behind an active session lane, exit with code 1, and print no actionable error even though the Gateway already logs the lane-wait facts.
- Solution: Propagate lane-wait facts from the command queue into CLI-tracked agent runs, return a structured Gateway final payload, and render a clear CLI busy message.
- What changed: Added typed queue wait info, an additive `rejectOnWait` queue hook, a session-lane-busy error helper, CLI-only embedded-runner opt-in, Gateway final payload handling, CLI stderr/JSON rendering, and focused regression coverage.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): No queue ordering change, no new CLI flag, no provider/channel/config/dependency change, and no change to non-CLI agent runs.

## Motivation

- Operators need to distinguish a blocked same-session lane from connection, model, or provider failures. The diagnostic data already exists; this patch makes the CLI caller receive it instead of a silent exit.

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #54622
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: A second CLI agent dispatch to the same session lane now receives a deterministic busy error when the first CLI dispatch keeps the lane occupied past the CLI timeout threshold.
- Real environment tested: Disposable Linux OpenClaw checkout on fresh `openclaw/openclaw` main `a547010a957546d3e1c573cf2fed6aac9295f315`, Node v24.15.0, repo-managed pnpm, local mock OpenAI endpoint, real Gateway service process, and two real source-entrypoint CLI `agent` processes.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
pnpm install --frozen-lockfile
git diff --check
pnpm exec oxfmt --check src/process/command-queue.types.ts src/process/command-queue.ts src/process/command-queue.test.ts src/agents/session-lane-busy.ts src/agents/command/types.ts src/agents/command/attempt-execution.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/params.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run.ts src/gateway/server-methods/agent.ts src/gateway/server-methods/agent.test.ts src/commands/agent-via-gateway.ts src/commands/agent-via-gateway.test.ts
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/process/command-queue.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/agent.test.ts src/commands/agent-via-gateway.test.ts
node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs --tsconfig config/tsconfig/oxlint.core.json src/process/command-queue.types.ts src/process/command-queue.ts src/process/command-queue.test.ts src/agents/session-lane-busy.ts src/agents/command/types.ts src/agents/command/attempt-execution.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/params.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run.ts src/gateway/server-methods/agent.ts src/gateway/server-methods/agent.test.ts src/commands/agent-via-gateway.ts src/commands/agent-via-gateway.test.ts
pnpm check:test-types
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/lanes.test.ts
node --import tsx src/entry.ts gateway run ...
node --import tsx src/entry.ts agent --agent hana --session-key agent:hana:main --message HOLD_LANE --timeout 10 --json
node --import tsx src/entry.ts agent --agent hana --session-key agent:hana:main --message SECOND_LANE --timeout 2
LANE_BUSY_HOLD_MS=35000 LANE_BUSY_PROVIDER_TIMEOUT_SECONDS=80 LANE_BUSY_FIRST_TIMEOUT_SECONDS=60 LANE_BUSY_SECOND_TIMEOUT_SECONDS=1 node lane-busy-cli-service-proof.mjs patched-long-active-provider80
```

- Evidence after fix:

```text
patched-v2 commit=a547010a957546d3e1c573cf2fed6aac9295f315
first CLI: exit=0, stdout contains FIRST_DONE
second CLI: exit=1, stdout empty
second CLI stderr:
Error: session lane busy (lane=session:agent:hana:main, waitedMs=4991ms, queueAhead=0, activeAhead=1, activeNow=0, queueBehind=0)
Suggestion: wait for the current task to complete and retry.
providerRequestCount=1
```

Long-active proof after the timer fix:

```text
patched-long-active-provider80
first CLI: exit=0, stdout contains FIRST_DONE
second CLI: exit=1, stdout empty
second CLI stderr:
Error: session lane busy (lane=session:agent:hana:main, waitedMs=999ms, queueAhead=0, activeAhead=1, activeNow=1, queueBehind=0)
Suggestion: wait for the current task to complete and retry.
providerRequestCount=1
```

- Observed result after fix: The second CLI dispatch fails before sending a second provider request, surfaces the lane as busy, and gives the operator a retry action.
- What was not tested: No external provider, channel, or built `dist` package binary was used. `openclaw.mjs --version` worked, but the Gateway/agent binary path needed `dist/entry.js`; `pnpm build` was killed by SIGKILL inside `tsdown` on the disposable test host. The real service proof therefore uses the source entrypoint (`node --import tsx src/entry.ts`). Full `pnpm check:changed` is not claimed because the local Crabbox binary failed its own sanity check, and a direct remote-child changed check exceeded the local 20 minute timeout during broad lint/type work.
- Before evidence:

```text
base-v2 commit=a547010a957546d3e1c573cf2fed6aac9295f315
first CLI: exit=0, stdout contains FIRST_DONE
second CLI: exit=0, stdout=SECOND_DONE
second CLI stderr empty
providerRequestCount=2

base-long-active-provider80
first CLI: exit=0, stdout contains FIRST_DONE
second CLI: no LANE_WAIT_EXCEEDED payload before Gateway final wait; Gateway timed out after 31000ms, then embedded fallback produced SECOND_DONE
providerRequestCount=3

queue before/after harness:
unpatched main: queuedTaskDidRun=true, queued outcome resolved queued-task-ran
patched branch: queuedTaskDidRun=false, queued outcome rejected AgentSessionLaneBusyError / LANE_WAIT_EXCEEDED
```

## Root Cause

- Root cause: The command queue already detects and logs lane waits, but that `onWait` path is diagnostic-only. CLI-tracked Gateway agent dispatches did not convert the wait event into a final response payload that the CLI relay could render.
- Missing detection / guardrail: There was no test that asserted a waited same-session CLI dispatch receives an actionable busy result instead of silently exiting.
- Contributing context (if known): The session lane serializer correctly protects same-session ordering; the missing piece was surfacing the existing wait state to the caller at the CLI timeout boundary.

## Regression Test Plan

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/process/command-queue.test.ts`, `src/gateway/server-methods/agent.test.ts`, and `src/commands/agent-via-gateway.test.ts`.
- Scenario the test should lock in: A queued same-lane entry is rejected at the wait threshold while the active lane remains blocked, without running the queued task; Gateway returns a structured `LANE_WAIT_EXCEEDED` final payload for CLI task tracking; the CLI relay renders a clear stderr message and exits non-zero.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: The queue, Gateway payload, and CLI relay are the three handoff points that previously dropped the lane-wait signal.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): Existing lane serialization tests cover ordering, but not caller-visible lane-busy propagation.
- If no new test is added, why not: New tests are included.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

A blocked same-session CLI agent dispatch now prints an actionable error instead of silently exiting:

```text
Error: session lane busy (...)
Suggestion: wait for the current task to complete and retry.
```

JSON-mode callers receive a structured lane-busy response with `errorCode: "LANE_WAIT_EXCEEDED"` and lane wait details.

## Diagram

```text
CLI #1 -> Gateway -> session lane active -> provider request in flight
CLI #2 -> Gateway -> same session lane waits -> CLI timeout threshold
      -> Gateway final payload LANE_WAIT_EXCEEDED
      -> CLI stderr busy message, exit 1, no second provider request
```

## Security Impact

- New permissions/capabilities? No.
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No.
- New/changed network calls? No; the patch prevents an extra provider call in the busy-lane case.
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No.
- Data access scope changed? No.
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: Not applicable.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux disposable test environment
- Runtime/container: Node v24.15.0, repo-managed pnpm
- Model/provider: Local mock OpenAI-compatible endpoint
- Integration/channel (if any): CLI agent relay through Gateway
- Relevant config (redacted): Token-auth local Gateway, one test agent, isolated temporary state, dummy provider key only

### Steps

1. Start a local Gateway service from the patched source entrypoint.
2. Dispatch CLI agent request #1 to `agent:hana:main` with a mock provider response delayed long enough to hold the lane.
3. Dispatch CLI agent request #2 to the same `agent:hana:main` session with a shorter timeout.
4. Compare base main and patched branch provider request counts and CLI output.

### Expected

- The second CLI dispatch reports the same-session lane as busy and exits non-zero without reaching the provider.

### Actual

- Patched branch: second CLI dispatch exits 1 with a session lane busy message and `providerRequestCount=1`.
- Unpatched main: second CLI dispatch waits, reaches the provider, returns `SECOND_DONE`, and `providerRequestCount=2`.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

```text
Focused tests:
Gateway agent tests: 302/302 PASS
Command queue tests: 32/32 PASS
CLI relay tests: 56/56 PASS
Adjacent embedded-agent-runner lanes tests: 6/6 PASS

Static checks:
git diff --check PASS
oxfmt changed files PASS
targeted type-aware oxlint PASS, 0 warnings, 0 errors
pnpm check:test-types PASS

Codex review:
Attempted locally before PR creation, but the Windows host denied executing codex.exe. No Codex-review green claim is made.
```

## Human Verification

- Verified scenarios: Base-vs-patched same-session CLI/Gateway service behavior, queue-level before/after behavior, Gateway final payload shape, CLI stderr rendering, CLI JSON lane-busy handling, formatting, lint, and test-types.
- Edge cases checked: The queued task is not executed after lane-busy rejection; the next queued entry can still drain after a rejected wait entry; the CLI opt-in uses the agent timeout threshold rather than the queue's ordinary warning threshold.
- What you did **not** verify: Full `pnpm check:changed`, built `dist` package binary, external providers, external channels, or a live user account.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

No review conversations exist yet.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Yes.
- Config/env changes? No.
- Migration needed? No.
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: Not applicable.

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: Normal short same-session waits could be rejected too aggressively.
  - Mitigation: The CLI opt-in uses the agent run timeout threshold, not the command queue's ordinary wait-warning threshold.
- Risk: Non-CLI agent surfaces could receive a new busy behavior unexpectedly.
  - Mitigation: `failOnSessionLaneWait` is an internal opt-in enabled for CLI task tracking only.
- Risk: Busy details could expose too much context.
  - Mitigation: The error includes lane identity and queue timing/counts only; it does not include prompts, provider payloads, secrets, or message content.

target.title: fix(control-ui): validate Telegram cron To against full target contract (#90467)
target.body:
## Summary

Adds save-time Control UI validation for the cron **announce → To** field when
the delivery channel is Telegram, so a cron job cannot be saved with a recipient
Telegram can never deliver to. Previously the announce `To` field accepted any
string and the failure only surfaced at runtime (issue #90467).

### What changed since the first revision

The first revision validated **numeric chat ids only**. That was too narrow:
cron announce delivery resolves the target through
`normalizeTargetForProvider("telegram", to)` → the Telegram plugin's
`normalizeTelegramMessagingTarget` (`extensions/telegram/src/targets.ts`,
`parseTelegramTarget` + `normalizeTelegramLookupTarget`), which accepts more than
numeric ids. A numeric-only check would have blocked valid recipients such as
`@mychat`, `https://t.me/mychannel`, and `telegram:123` before save.

Validation now mirrors the full delivery contract:

- numeric chat ids (`-1001234567890`, `123456789`)
- `@username` and bare usernames (`[A-Za-z0-9_]{5,}`)
- `t.me` links (`t.me/...`, `https://t.me/...`)
- `telegram:` / `tg:` prefixes
- optional `:topicId` / `:topic:topicId` suffix on any of the above

Anything else (e.g. a phone number from the generic placeholder, or a hyphenated
name) is rejected before save. The Telegram plugin remains the canonical owner of
the contract; the UI regex is a documented save-time UX mirror, not a second
source of truth (the UI package cannot import plugin internals across the
`extensions/**` boundary).

Also in this PR:
- Telegram-specific placeholder/help copy on the announce `To` field.
- Inline error rendering + `aria-invalid` / `aria-describedby` on the announce
  `To` field (previously only the webhook field rendered its error).
- Regenerated i18n locale bundles + `.i18n` metadata for the new English strings
  (`pnpm ui:i18n:sync`), so the UI i18n drift gate stays green.

## Verification

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs ui/src/ui/controllers/cron.test.ts` → 43 passed
  (new cases: rejects phone/hyphenated/short-handle targets; accepts every
  Telegram form runtime resolves; ignores the format on non-Telegram channels).
- `node --import tsx scripts/control-ui-i18n.ts check` → all locales clean.
- `oxfmt --check` and `oxlint` clean on the changed source files.

### Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed:** cron announce `To` for Telegram now blocks unsendable
  targets at save time and accepts every form Telegram delivery resolves.
- **Real environment tested:** local Vitest (Control UI controller) + i18n gate.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**
  `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs ui/src/ui/controllers/cron.test.ts`;
  `node --import tsx scripts/control-ui-i18n.ts check`.
- **Evidence after fix:** 43/43 controller tests pass; i18n check reports all
  locales clean.
- **Observed result after fix:** valid Telegram targets save; invalid ones show
  the inline `telegramChatIdInvalid` error.
- **What was not tested:** browser-rendered screenshot of the cron form
  helper/error states (logic is covered by controller unit tests; a maintainer
  can request Mantis visual proof if required before merge).

Closes #90467

target.title: feat: add chain-of-thought pre-flight planning for long-running goals
target.body:
# Pull Request Walkthrough: Chain-of-Thought (CoT) Pre-Flight Planning for Long-Running Goals

This PR adds a structured Chain-of-Thought (CoT) pre-flight planning system to OpenClaw agents. The planning system activates when a long-running goal is created, guiding the agent through explicit task decomposition, dependency analysis, and checkpoint planning before execution begins. The progress of the plan is tracked and updated as execution proceeds and is preserved across compaction handoffs.

---

## Key Changes

### 1. Types & Schema
- **File**: [types.ts](file:///Volumes/AI%20Project%20Storage/OpenClawPR/src/config/sessions/types.ts)
  - Defined `SessionGoalPlanStep` representing individual steps with status (`pending`, `active`, `done`, etc.), descriptions, estimated tokens, checkpoints, and dependencies (`dependsOn`).
  - Defined `SessionGoalPlanSnapshot` to store the active goal plan strategy, risks, and checkpoints.
  - Attached `planSnapshot?: SessionGoalPlanSnapshot` to the existing `SessionGoal` session type.

### 2. Configuration Settings
- **File**: [types.agent-defaults.ts](file:///Volumes/AI%20Project%20Storage/OpenClawPR/src/config/types.agent-defaults.ts)
  - Added `cotPlanning` default options under `AgentDefaultsConfig` supporting `mode` (`auto`, `always`, `off`) and `minBudgetTokens` (default `50000`).

### 3. Prompt Layer Integration
- **File**: [cot-planning-prompt.ts](file:///Volumes/AI%20Project%20Storage/OpenClawPR/src/agents/cot-planning-prompt.ts) (NEW)
  - Implemented `shouldActivateCotPlanning` to auto-trigger planning for goals with large budgets or when configured.
  - Implemented `buildCotPlanningSystemPromptSection` which instructs models to emit a structured plan inside `<cot_plan>` tags.
  - Implemented `parseCotPlanResponse` to parse XML-like plan outputs.
  - Implemented `buildCotPlanProgressSection` to track and display active/pending/done plan steps in subsequent turns.
- **File**: [system-prompt.ts](file:///Volumes/AI%20Project%20Storage/OpenClawPR/src/agents/system-prompt.ts)
  - Hooked planning prompts into `buildAgentSystemPrompt` inside the stable prefix boundary (before `SYSTEM_PROMPT_CACHE_BOUNDARY`) to maintain cache stability.

### 4. Planning & Execution Tools
- **File**: [cot-planning-tools.ts](file:///Volumes/AI%20Project%20Storage/OpenClawPR/src/agents/tools/cot-planning-tools.ts) (NEW)
  - Implemented `update_goal_plan` to attach or replace a structured plan.
  - Implemented `update_goal_plan_step` to modify a step's execution status (e.g. marking a step as `done`, `active`, or `blocked` with comments).
- **File**: [goal-tools.ts](file:///Volumes/AI%20Project%20Storage/OpenClawPR/src/agents/tools/goal-tools.ts)
  - Extended `create_goal` to accept an optional pre-defined `plan` snapshot during creation.

### 5. Transcript Compaction & Handoff
- **File**: [compaction.ts](file:///Volumes/AI%20Project%20Storage/OpenClawPR/src/agents/compaction.ts)
  - Updated `HANDOFF_INSTRUCTIONS` to direct the compaction summarizer to preserve the active CoT plan status (strategy, completed/active/pending steps, and next actionable step) for context continuity during model handoffs.

---

## Verification Plan

### Automated Tests
The following unit test suites have been executed and are **100% passing**:
1. **Prompt Generator & Parser Tests**: [cot-planning-prompt.test.ts](file:///Volumes/AI%20Project%20Storage/OpenClawPR/src/agents/cot-planning-prompt.test.ts) (7/7 tests passed)
   - Checks activation conditions (mode, budgets, existing plans).
   - Verifies system prompt layout for planning instruction and progress tracking.
   - Tests XML parsing for well-formed and malformed model outputs.
2. **Tool Execution Tests**: [cot-planning-tools.test.ts](file:///Volumes/AI%20Project%20Storage/OpenClawPR/src/agents/tools/cot-planning-tools.test.ts) (8/8 tests passed)
   - Verifies `update_goal_plan` creates plans, updates step states, validates dependencies/IDs, and errors correctly when goals are missing/inactive.
3. **Goal Creation Plan Integration Tests**: [goal-tools.test.ts](file:///Volumes/AI%20Project%20Storage/OpenClawPR/src/agents/tools/goal-tools.test.ts) (8/8 tests passed)
   - Verifies plan snapshots can be directly populated during goal creation.

To run tests:
```bash
npx vitest src/agents/cot-planning-prompt.test.ts --run
npx vitest src/agents/tools/cot-planning-tools.test.ts --run
npx vitest src/agents/tools/goal-tools.test.ts --run
```

---

## Demo & Visual Proof

Below is the recording of the browser subagent running through the 8-step pre-flight planning checklist and generating the finalized plan:

![CoT Pre-Flight Planning Demo Recording](/Users/gregoryhall/.gemini/antigravity-ide/brain/7acd7c1c-4800-4ebd-b923-fd336d12174c/cot_planning_demo_1780694993924.webp)

And here is the initial state of the Pre-Flight Builder interface:

![Pre-Flight Builder Initial State](/Users/gregoryhall/.gemini/antigravity-ide/brain/7acd7c1c-4800-4ebd-b923-fd336d12174c/step_1_initial_1780694997940.png)

target.title: fix(msteams): read file attachments on Teams channel messages (team GUID + channel fallback + thread-reply URL)
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes inbound **file attachments on Microsoft Teams channel messages** (`@openclaw/msteams`). Three independent bugs meant the agent could never read a file attached in a channel — and for thread replies it silently returned the *thread root's* file instead. Detailed root-cause write-up in #90733 (closed as a duplicate of this issue). All three confirmed present on `main` and v2026.6.1.

### 1. Wrong team id → Graph `400`
`buildMSTeamsGraphMessageUrls` built `/teams/{team-id}/…` from `channelData.team.id`, which is the `19:…@thread.tacv2` thread id — not the team's AAD group GUID. The Graph `/teams/{id}` path needs `channelData.team.aadGroupId`. Now prefers `aadGroupId` (with the old fields as fallback).

### 2. Graph fetch never attempted for channel @mentions
The fetch was gated on `hasHtmlFileAttachment` (an `<attachment id>` HTML marker). Channel @mention activities deliver only a `text/html` stub without that marker, so the fetch was skipped even though the file is fetchable via Graph. Now attempts the Graph message fetch for non-personal conversations regardless of the marker.

### 3. Thread replies fetched the wrong message
A reply was fetched at `/messages/{replyId}` (404 — a reply id is not a top-level message) then fell back to `/messages/{root}` (the thread root), returning the **root's** attachment for every reply. A reply is addressable only at `/messages/{root}/replies/{replyId}`. Now treats any candidate that isn't the current message id as a potential parent, builds the reply URL first, and only fetches `/messages/{selfId}` for the message itself — never the bare root.

### Changes
- `extensions/msteams/src/attachments/graph.ts` — team GUID resolution; channel reply-URL construction.
- `extensions/msteams/src/monitor-handler/inbound-media.ts` — attempt the channel Graph fetch without an HTML marker.
- `extensions/msteams/src/attachments.helpers.test.ts` — regression test (aadGroupId + reply-under-root, never the bare root).
- `extensions/msteams/src/monitor-handler/inbound-media.test.ts` — the mention-only case now expects the Graph fallback for channel/group threads.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: reading file attachments (PDF + audio) from Microsoft Teams channel messages — top-level and thread replies. Before the fix the agent reported "no attachment" on top-level messages and returned the thread root's file on replies.

Real environment tested: a live Microsoft Teams tenant on a deployed OpenClaw agent, plus direct Microsoft Graph calls with the bot app token (Graph application permission `ChannelMessage.Read.All` admin-consented; manifest RSC `ChannelMessage.Read.Group`).

Exact steps or command run after this patch: @mentioned the bot in a channel with a file attached on (1) a top-level message and (2) a thread reply, for both a PDF and an mp3 — the agent fetched and read/transcribed the correct file each time. Also issued the four Graph calls shown below directly with the bot token to confirm the URL shapes.

Evidence after fix: redacted runtime console output from the live agent's plugin (a debug probe added during diagnosis), for a PDF attached as a thread reply — the corrected reply URL is built first and Graph returns the reply's own attachment (`200`, `attachmentCount:1`):

```
[RADAR-DBG] ids {"selfId":"1780683115056","conversationMessageId":"1780681398985","parents":["1780681398985"]}
[RADAR-DBG] graphUrls ["https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/teams/<aadGroupId>/channels/<chan>/messages/1780681398985/replies/1780683115056",
                       "https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/teams/<aadGroupId>/channels/<chan>/messages/1780683115056"]
[RADAR-DBG] graphMedia {"mediaLen":1,"attempts":[{"url":".../messages/1780681398985/replies/1780683115056","hostedStatus":200,"attachmentStatus":200,"attachmentCount":1}]}
```

Direct Microsoft Graph calls with the bot app token, confirming each URL shape (redacted host/ids):

```
GET /teams/{team.id == 19:..@thread.tacv2}/channels/{c}/messages/{id}     -> 400  (invalid team id)
GET /teams/{aadGroupId}/channels/{c}/messages/{topLevelId}                -> 200  (+ attachment)
GET /teams/{aadGroupId}/channels/{c}/messages/{replyId}                   -> 404  (reply is not a top-level message)
GET /teams/{aadGroupId}/channels/{c}/messages/{rootId}/replies/{replyId}  -> 200  (+ the reply's actual attachment)
```

Observed result after fix: the correct file is read every time — for replies and top-level, documents and audio. A top-level PDF, a thread-reply PDF, and a thread-reply mp3 were each read/transcribed correctly; previously all returned the thread-root file.

What was not tested: the local `vitest` / `oxfmt` / `tsgo` suite was not run on my machine (developed against a release checkout; behavior verified live and via direct Graph calls instead). Please confirm CI — happy to adjust to maintainer preference.

Fixes #89594

target.title: fix(ports): batch macOS lsof listener inspection to one spawn per cycle
target.body:
## Summary

Refs #90548. This is a partial mitigation and intentionally does not close the issue.

This changes Unix listener inspection from one `lsof` spawn per inspected port to one LISTEN snapshot per same-cycle multi-port inspection, then filters the snapshot back into the existing per-port `PortUsage` shape.

Follow-up repairs on current head preserve the pre-existing single-port `inspectPortUsage(port)` command shape and fix the daemon CLI coverage mock for the new batch helper:

- one-port callers stay scoped to `lsof -nP -iTCP:<port> -sTCP:LISTEN -FpFcn`
- explicit multi-port `inspectPortUsages([...])` calls use the shared all-LISTEN snapshot
- `src/cli/daemon-cli.coverage.test.ts` now exports/mocks `inspectPortUsages`, returns a `Map<number, PortUsage>`, clears it between cases, and asserts the daemon/CLI differing-port status path uses the batch helper

## Affected Surface

- `src/infra/ports-inspect.ts`
  - `readUnixListenersFromLsof`
  - `readUnixListenerSnapshot`
  - `inspectPortUsages`
  - `recordsForPort`
- `src/infra/ports.ts`
  - exports `inspectPortUsages`
- `src/cli/daemon-cli/status.gather.ts`
  - batches daemon/CLI port status inspection when the two ports differ
- `src/cli/daemon-cli.coverage.test.ts`
  - updates the `../infra/ports.js` mock and assertions for the new batch helper

## Scope

Changed:

- Unix LISTEN inspection now supports a shared snapshot command for explicit batch calls:
  - `lsof -nP -iTCP -sTCP:LISTEN -FpFcn`
- Single-port Unix LISTEN inspection remains targeted:
  - `lsof -nP -iTCP:<port> -sTCP:LISTEN -FpFcn`
- The snapshot parser preserves pid, command, address, and per-port filtered detail.
- `inspectDaemonPortStatuses` uses the batch helper for daemon/CLI same-cycle checks.
- The daemon CLI coverage mock now includes `inspectPortUsages` so the status path imports and exercises the new named export correctly.
- `checkPortInUse` still runs per port when no matching listener is found.

Intentionally not changed:

- Windows netstat inspection remains separate.
- Linux `ss` fallback remains separate and per-port when lsof fails.
- `inspectPortConnections` / ESTABLISHED connection inspection is untouched.
- The public per-port `PortUsage` result shape is preserved.
- Per-port `detail` does not include unrelated sockets from the all-port lsof snapshot.
- Restart-health polling remains outside this PR, so #90548 should stay open for that remaining surface.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** macOS Unix listener inspection previously spawned `lsof` once per inspected port. The patched multi-port helper inspects multiple same-cycle listener ports with one all-LISTEN `lsof` snapshot and still returns filtered per-port `PortUsage` results. The follow-up repair keeps existing single-port callers targeted to the requested port, so restart-health and other one-port diagnostics do not inherit the all-listener scan. `gateway status` wiring is covered by status collector and daemon CLI coverage regression tests that call `inspectPortUsages([daemonPort, cliPort])` when daemon and CLI ports differ.

**Real environment tested:** local Mac mini4 development machine on `darwin`, branch `wolf/ports-inspect-batch-lsof-listeners`, head `917ef0d4161abc29e93419fb5af560c34b285bb8`. The live proof used two temporary local TCP listeners bound to `127.0.0.1` on ephemeral ports. No secrets, production services, or private traffic were used.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

```sh
# Live macOS behavior proof: start two local TCP listeners, call the real branch helpers,
# and verify both single-port and batch APIs return busy listener details.
node --import tsx --input-type=module

# Focused regression tests and checks on the same head.
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cli/daemon-cli.coverage.test.ts
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cli/daemon-cli.coverage.test.ts src/cli/daemon-cli/status.gather.test.ts src/infra/ports.test.ts
./node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/cli/daemon-cli.coverage.test.ts src/cli/daemon-cli/status.gather.ts src/cli/daemon-cli/status.gather.test.ts src/infra/ports-inspect.ts src/infra/ports.ts src/infra/ports.test.ts
git diff --check origin/main..HEAD
gitleaks protect --staged --redact
gitleaks git --redact --log-opts origin/main..HEAD
```

**Evidence after fix:** copied live macOS terminal output from current head:

```json
{
  "platform": "darwin",
  "head": "917ef0d4161abc29e93419fb5af560c34b285bb8",
  "ports": [52944, 52945],
  "single": {
    "port": 52944,
    "status": "busy",
    "listenerCount": 1,
    "detailMentionsRequestedPort": true
  },
  "batch": [
    {
      "port": 52944,
      "status": "busy",
      "listenerCount": 1,
      "detailMentionsRequestedPort": true
    },
    {
      "port": 52945,
      "status": "busy",
      "listenerCount": 1,
      "detailMentionsRequestedPort": true
    }
  ]
}
```

Focused validation after fix:

- RED proof before the single-port repair: the new single-port command-shape test failed because the earlier PR head used `-iTCP` instead of `-iTCP:18789`.
- RED proof before the daemon CLI coverage mock repair: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cli/daemon-cli.coverage.test.ts` failed in `derives probe URL from service args + env (json)` with `__exit__:1` because the mock did not export the newly imported `inspectPortUsages` batch helper.
- GREEN proof after the daemon CLI coverage mock repair: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cli/daemon-cli.coverage.test.ts` passed, 1 file, 7/7 tests.
- Combined targeted run after the mock repair: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cli/daemon-cli.coverage.test.ts src/cli/daemon-cli/status.gather.test.ts src/infra/ports.test.ts` passed, 3 files, 51/51 tests.
- Targeted `oxfmt --check`: all 6 matched files use correct format.
- `git diff --check origin/main..HEAD`: passed.
- `gitleaks protect --staged --redact`: no leaks found.
- `gitleaks git --redact --log-opts origin/main..HEAD`: no leaks found.

Command-shape regression coverage:

- `inspectPortUsage(18789)` now asserts exactly one mocked `lsof` invocation with args `-nP -iTCP:18789 -sTCP:LISTEN -FpFcn`.
- `inspectPortUsages([18789, 19001])` still asserts exactly one mocked `lsof` invocation with args `-nP -iTCP -sTCP:LISTEN -FpFcn` and verifies per-port filtering excludes an unrelated port `3000` listener from requested-port details.
- The status collector regression verifies daemon/CLI port status inspection calls `inspectPortUsages([19001, 18789])` and does not call the per-port `inspectPortUsage` path when the ports differ.
- The daemon CLI coverage regression now uses the same batch-helper boundary in its mock and affected assertions.

**Observed result after fix:** on the live macOS proof run, both `inspectPortUsage(52944)` and `inspectPortUsages([52944, 52945])` returned `busy` listener results for real local TCP listeners, with each per-port detail mentioning the requested listener port. Focused tests confirmed the command boundary: single-port checks stay scoped to `-iTCP:<port>`, while explicit multi-port inspection still uses one shared all-LISTEN snapshot. The daemon CLI coverage test now imports the status path successfully and verifies the batch helper is used when daemon and CLI ports differ.

**What was not tested:**

- I did not reproduce launchservicesd / WindowServer saturation. That scenario is disruptive and risks destabilizing the desktop session.
- I did not start or mutate a real OpenClaw LaunchAgent service.
- I did not cover restart-health batching in this PR. Restart-health now keeps its existing targeted single-port lsof behavior; because the remaining repeated health-loop surface is still open, this PR uses `Refs #90548` rather than a closing reference.

## De-duplication Evidence

Immediately before the original push, these five independent open-PR searches returned no results:

- `Fixes #90548`
- `#90548`
- `ports-inspect lsof iTCP`
- `inspectPortUsage readUnixListeners`
- `launchservicesd lsof port`

## Narrow Fix Definition

1. This is a shared helper/default-policy gap: the Unix port-inspection helper owned a per-port process-spawn strategy.
2. Fixing only one visible caller would leave adjacent status/diagnostic paths able to repeat the same per-port lsof pattern, so the fix is helper-level plus batch-aware status caller wiring.
3. Maintainers should expect a canonical shared helper fix rather than a local status-only patch because the bug is process-spawn strategy, not output formatting.
4. Supersession risk is lower with this shared boundary: a narrower macOS-app-only or status-only patch would be more likely to be replaced.

target.title: feat(slack): add workspace member policy
target.body:
## Summary

- add `channels.slack.memberPolicy` so Slack can fail closed to full workspace members before dispatch
- cache `users.info` access metadata and apply the policy to normal inbound messages plus Slack system/interactive events
- document the new config and cover schema, account merge, and policy decisions with tests

## Policy behavior

When enabled, the policy defaults to:

- require the sender `team_id` to match the workspace returned by `auth.test`
- deny restricted and ultra-restricted guests
- deny Slack Connect/external strangers
- deny bot/app users
- deny deleted users
- fail closed if `users.info` cannot verify the sender

Example:

```json
{
  "channels": {
    "slack": {
      "memberPolicy": {
        "enabled": true,
        "denyGuests": true,
        "denyExternal": true,
        "denyBots": true
      }
    }
  }
}
```

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed: Slack inbound authorization can now be restricted to real full members of the connected workspace instead of allowing guests, Slack Connect strangers, bots/apps, deleted users, or users from another team through the normal Slack message/action dispatch path.

Real environment tested: A live OpenClaw installation connected to a production Slack workspace through the Slack plugin. User IDs and names are redacted; only aggregate counts are included.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran the live Slack directory command and evaluated the returned Slack user metadata with this PR's default `memberPolicy` decisions (`team_id` match, deny deleted users, bots/apps, restricted/ultra-restricted guests, and Slack Connect strangers).

```bash
openclaw directory peers list --channel slack --json
```

Evidence after fix: Redacted live output from the real OpenClaw/Slack setup:

```json
{
  "checkedAt": "2026-06-05T09:44:06.230Z",
  "expectedTeam": "T026RK84A",
  "total": 454,
  "allowedWorkspaceHumans": 46,
  "denied": {
    "deleted": 165,
    "bot": 102,
    "guest": 141,
    "external": 0,
    "teamMismatch": 0,
    "missingTeam": 0
  },
  "sampleAllowedIds": [
    "redacted",
    "redacted",
    "redacted",
    "redacted",
    "redacted"
  ]
}
```

Observed result after fix: The policy keeps 46 full workspace humans eligible and excludes 141 guest accounts plus non-human/deleted Slack directory entries from the real workspace. No external/Slack Connect strangers or team mismatches were present in this workspace at the time of the live check.

What was not tested: No known gaps.

## Tests

- `pnpm exec vitest run extensions/slack/src/monitor/member-policy.test.ts extensions/slack/src/config-schema.test.ts extensions/slack/src/accounts.test.ts --config vitest.config.ts`
- `pnpm tsgo:extensions`

target.title: Speed up precomputed command help startup
target.body:
## Summary
- add strict launcher/entry fast paths for precomputed `doctor`, `gateway`, `models`, `plugins`, `sessions`, and `tasks` help
- keep container routing in control for those precomputed subcommand help paths
- fix Windows `node --import` RSS hook paths in startup benchmark tooling

AI-assisted: yes. I understand what this code does: it consumes existing precomputed subcommand help metadata earlier in the launcher/entry startup path, while preserving runtime fallthrough for container-sensitive help.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: `openclaw doctor --help`, `openclaw gateway --help`, `openclaw models --help`, `openclaw plugins --help`, `openclaw sessions --help`, and `openclaw tasks --help` now return precomputed help from the launcher/entry fast path instead of importing the full runtime before printing help.

Real environment tested: Windows 11 local OpenClaw checkout in `C:\Users\Yeqiu\Documents\agent-windows-lab\worktrees\openclaw-perf`, Node v24.9.0, pnpm toolchain from the repo, running the built CLI benchmark against the patched branch.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
```powershell
node --import tsx scripts/bench-cli-startup.ts --case doctorHelp --case gatewayHelp --case modelsHelp --case pluginsHelp --runs 3 --warmup 1 --timeout-ms 10000 --output .artifacts/openclaw-startup-final-after-review.json
node --import tsx scripts/bench-cli-startup.ts --case sessionsHelp --case tasksHelp --runs 5 --warmup 1 --json --output .artifacts\cli-startup-more-final-after-entry.json
```

Evidence after fix: copied live terminal output from benchmark runs:
```text
doctor --help: avg=50.5ms firstOutputAvg=42.1ms rssAvg=46.1MB code=0x3
gateway --help: avg=59.1ms firstOutputAvg=49.7ms rssAvg=46.2MB code=0x3
```

Observed result after fix: All six changed direct subcommand help invocations exited successfully and printed first output in about 42-52 ms for the first four commands and about 45-48 ms for `sessions`/`tasks`. Earlier local fallback measurements for `sessions --help` and `tasks --help` were about 1.6-1.8s with about 253-256 MB RSS, so these help-only invocations now avoid the full runtime import.

What was not tested: Full cross-platform CI matrix locally. Focused launcher/runtime behavior, container fallthrough, startup benchmark tooling, and the copied live benchmark output above were tested locally. GitHub CI is rerunning after the latest push.

target.title: fix #87700: [Bug]: Control UI webchat session silently resets after network disconnect / sleep — previous session file renamed to .jsonl.reset.* and lost from sessions tab
target.body:
## Summary

What problem does this PR solve?

- Fix classification: root-cause fix for a scoped Control UI reconnect/session-lifecycle bug.
- Root cause: the session lifecycle state source was split after a Control UI sleep/network reconnect: the UI continued to display the previous `sessionId`, while Gateway freshness handling treated the next send like an ordinary stale send and rotated that matching transcript into a reset archive before the resumed turn landed.
- Why this is root-cause fix: the source invariant is now enforced at the owner boundaries: Control UI creates a one-shot private reconnect marker only for the displayed matching session, Gateway accepts that marker only from operator UI clients, and reply initialization receives the resume request only after public protocol validation remains closed.
- The reconnect recovery is now scoped to Control UI behavior instead of publishing a new general `chat.send` resume contract or Plugin SDK reply-options surface.

Why does this matter now?

- The linked bug is user-visible session loss after an ordinary Control UI reconnect.
- The earlier shape fixed that path by adding `chat.send.resumeSession`, but that made the fix look like a new public Gateway protocol feature and required a broader product/session-policy decision.

What is the intended outcome?

- A Control UI reconnect send can preserve the currently visible matching session for that one send.
- Ordinary stale sends still follow configured idle/daily reset freshness and create a fresh `sessionId` when policy requires it.
- `chat.send.resumeSession` is not part of the public Gateway protocol schema, generated Swift model, or docs.
- `requestedSessionId` / `resumeRequestedSession` stay on the internal Gateway/reply path and are not part of exported Plugin SDK `GetReplyOptions`.

What is intentionally out of scope?

- This does not introduce a third-party reconnect/resume API.
- This does not change session reset configuration, daily reset timing, idle freshness evaluation, transcript storage, or migration behavior.

What does success look like?

- Control UI reconnect recovery remains available for the original user path.
- Public WebChat / non-operator clients cannot use the private Control UI marker as a generic session freshness override.
- Protocol validation rejects `resumeSession` as an extra `chat.send` property.
- Plugin SDK `reply-runtime` keeps `GetReplyOptions` free of the private reconnect resume controls.

What should reviewers focus on?

- The product boundary: reconnect resume is now an internal Control UI affordance, not a published Gateway protocol field or Plugin SDK option.
- The session-state boundary: the one-shot resume still requires a matching `sessionId`; ordinary stale sends still rotate.

<details>
<summary>Summary guidance</summary>

This PR description is the contributor's durable explanation of the change. Write it for human maintainers first; ClawSweeper and Barnacle use the same text to understand intent, proof, risk, and current review state.

Describe the intent and outcome in 2-5 bullets. Avoid restating the diff; reviewers and bots can read the changed files.

If this PR fixes a plugin beta-release blocker, title it `fix(<plugin-id>): beta blocker - <summary>` and link the matching `Beta blocker: <plugin-name> - <summary>` issue labeled `beta-blocker`. Contributors cannot label PRs, so the title is the PR-side signal for maintainers and automation.

</details>

## Linked context

Which issue does this close?

Closes #87700

Which issues, PRs, or discussions are related?

Related #89017

Was this requested by a maintainer or owner?

No direct maintainer request; ClawSweeper requested maintainer/product review for the public protocol shape, so this update narrows the shape to avoid a public API decision.

<details>
<summary>Linked context guidance</summary>

Link the issue, PR, discussion, maintainer request, or owner request that explains why this PR should exist. Maintainer context helps reviewers and automation distinguish intended work from drive-by churn.

</details>

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: Control UI reconnect send preserves the matching visible session without exposing a public `chat.send.resumeSession` field.
- Real environment tested: Foreground Gateway websocket RPC from the current checkout, using real Control UI and public WebChat client identities with browser-origin and device-identity handshake requirements enabled.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  - Started a foreground dev Gateway from the current checkout with isolated state.
  - Seeded stale session registry rows for the ordinary-send and reconnect-send cases.
  - Sent real `chat.send` websocket RPCs for Control UI reconnect, public WebChat with the private marker, and ordinary stale Control UI send.
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output): Validation `live-control-ui-reconnect-proof` (pass, exit_code=0, 88836ms):

  ```text
  LIVE_GATEWAY_PROOF: PASS foreground Gateway websocket RPC exercised #87700/#89017 behavior
  scenario=control-ui-reconnect-send result=PASS requested=reconnect-preserved-session observed=reconnect-preserved-session behavior=preserved_same_visible_session_after_reconnect
  scenario=public-webchat-private-marker result=PASS rpc_ok=false error=invalid_chat_send_params behavior=private_marker_rejected_for_non_operator_client
  scenario=ordinary-stale-control-ui-send result=PASS requested=ordinary-old-session observed=a6b07d66-eb19-42fc-b4c3-fbf293e9b7a1 behavior=rotated_by_stale_freshness
  LIVE_GATEWAY_PROOF: PASS public protocol stayed closed while Control UI reconnect recovery worked
  ```

- Observed result after fix:
  - Control UI reconnect send with the private marker kept the same visible session id.
  - Public WebChat sending the private Control UI reconnect marker was rejected by `chat.send` validation.
  - Ordinary stale Control UI send without the reconnect marker rotated to a new session id.
  - `resumeSession` remains outside the public `chat.send` protocol contract.
- What was not tested: None for the scoped Gateway/session behavior covered by this proof.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: The live proof targets the runtime session boundary that caused #87700; Control UI marker construction is covered by the supplemental controller regression tests below.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): Before the scoped reconnect handling, a stale send with the displayed matching session was handled like an ordinary stale send and rotated instead of preserving the visible reconnect session.

<details>
<summary>Real behavior proof guidance</summary>

External contributors must show after-fix evidence from a real OpenClaw setup. Unit tests, mocks, lint, typechecks, snapshots, and CI are supplemental only.

Screenshots are encouraged even for CLI, console, text, or log changes. Terminal screenshots, copied live output, redacted runtime logs, recordings, and linked artifacts count.

If your environment cannot produce the ideal proof, explain that under `Proof limitations or environment constraints` so reviewers and ClawSweeper can direct the next step properly.

Be mindful of private information like IP addresses, API keys, phone numbers, non-public endpoints, or other private details when providing evidence.

</details>

## Tests and validation

Which commands did you run?

- `vitest run src/plugins/contracts/plugin-sdk-subpaths.test.ts src/gateway/server.chat.gateway-server-chat-b.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/session.test.ts` (pass, exit_code=0, 319160ms)
- `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.json --noEmit` (pass, exit_code=0, 370255ms)
- `oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/auto-reply/get-reply-options.types.ts src/auto-reply/reply/get-reply.types.ts src/auto-reply/reply/get-reply.ts src/auto-reply/dispatch.ts src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-from-config.types.ts src/gateway/test-helpers.runtime-state.ts src/gateway/server.chat.gateway-server-chat-b.test.ts src/plugins/contracts/plugin-sdk-subpaths.test.ts` (pass, exit_code=0, 1141ms)
- `git diff --check` (pass, exit_code=0, 126ms)

What regression coverage was added or updated?

- Protocol validator coverage proves `resumeSession` is not a public `chat.send` parameter.
- Gateway websocket coverage proves the private reconnect marker is accepted only for Control UI and rejected for public WebChat.
- Plugin SDK contract coverage proves `openclaw/plugin-sdk/reply-runtime` does not expose `requestedSessionId` / `resumeRequestedSession` through `GetReplyOptions`.
- Control UI controller coverage proves ordinary sends do not include any resume marker and reconnect sends use the private marker once.

What failed before this fix, if known?

- The protocol regression failed before the schema update because `validateChatSendParams(...)` accepted `resumeSession: true`.

If no test was added, why not?

- Tests were updated for protocol, Gateway websocket, and Control UI request behavior.

<details>
<summary>Testing guidance</summary>

List focused commands, not every incidental check. CI is useful support, but external PRs still need real behavior proof above when behavior changes.

</details>

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

Yes. Control UI reconnect sends can preserve the matching visible session once; this is the intended fix for the linked session-loss path.

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

No.

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

Yes, in the limited sense that Gateway request validation now has an internal Control UI-only marker. Non-operator/public clients are rejected by validation coverage.

What is the highest-risk area?

Session-state and protocol boundary: avoiding a generic client-controlled override of idle/daily freshness.

How is that risk mitigated?

- `resumeSession` was removed from the public schema and generated Swift model.
- `requestedSessionId` / `resumeRequestedSession` were removed from exported `GetReplyOptions` and kept on internal Gateway/reply option types.
- The private marker is stripped before validation only for operator UI clients.
- Public WebChat coverage proves the marker is rejected for non-Control UI clients.
- Reply initialization still only resumes when the supplied `sessionId` matches the current session entry.

Related open PR / competition scan / contract scope:

- This update is scoped to the existing author PR #89017 for #87700; it does not open a competing public-protocol PR or ask maintainers to accept a new public Gateway field.
- Same-contract check: the canonical source of truth remains the Gateway protocol schema; public clients get no `resumeSession` property, and the generated Swift model no longer exposes one.
- Compatibility boundary: because the public field was only proposed in this unmerged PR, removing it avoids creating a new external client contract rather than breaking a released one.

Patch quality warnings / notes:

- Patch quality warning, diff size: the PR necessarily spans Control UI request construction, Gateway request validation, session initialization, generated protocol model, docs, and regression tests so the contract is narrowed consistently; splitting would leave either the UI marker, protocol schema, or Gateway behavior inconsistent.
- Patch quality warning, default return: `src/gateway/server.chat.gateway-server-chat-b.test.ts` returns `undefined` from the mocked reply only to stop the harness after capturing reply initialization options; this is test-only control flow, not a production fallback.
- Patch quality warning, type escape: the `unknown` casts are confined to UI helper/test setup where the shared UI state object is intentionally sliced down to chat-controller fields; they do not widen the runtime `chat.send` payload or relax Gateway validation.

<details>
<summary>Risk guidance</summary>

Use this for author judgment that is not obvious from the diff. ClawSweeper can see touched files, but it cannot know which behavior you think is risky, why the risk is acceptable, or what mitigation reviewers should verify.

</details>

## Current review state

What is the next action?

Review the narrowed implementation and let CI settle after this update.

target.title: feat(feeds): add feed lifecycle tooling
target.body:
## Summary

Adds feed publisher/subscriber lifecycle tooling on top of the feed source and install primitives.

This PR adds:

- `openclaw feeds validate <path-or-url>` and `feeds hash <path-or-url>`.
- `openclaw feeds build --inventory <path> --out <path>` for deterministic curated feed artifacts.
- `openclaw feeds diff --previous <path> --current <path>` for publisher-side delta review.
- `openclaw feeds updates --installed <path>` to compare installed skill/plugin inventory against configured feeds.
- `openclaw feeds notices --installed <path>` for subscriber-facing update notices with install commands.
- Semver-aware update ordering, including prerelease vs stable comparisons.

## Example publisher flow

```bash
openclaw feeds validate ./company-feed.json --json
openclaw feeds hash ./company-feed.json
openclaw feeds build --inventory ./inventory.json --out ./company-feed.json --id company-approved
openclaw feeds diff --previous ./previous-feed.json --current ./company-feed.json --json
```

## Example subscriber flow

```bash
openclaw feeds updates --installed ./installed.json --approved-only --json
openclaw feeds notices --installed ./installed.json --approved-only --json
```

## Notes

This stays file-based and explicit. There is no daemon, background watch, auto-update, or ClawHub-native feed service in this PR. A later RFC/PR can add ClawHub-native feed support once the local feed contract is stable.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed:
Feed lifecycle CLI validates and hashes feed URL inputs, builds deterministic artifacts by default, diffs feed artifacts, and reports subscriber updates/notices.

Real environment tested:
WSL Ubuntu 24.04, source checkout `/root/src/openclaw-feeds-87827` at `47aabc7bcd2ec3289fed0b4cbf4e6834cf385133`, source CLI via `node scripts/run-node.mjs`, temporary OpenClaw config/state/home, and local `file://` feed artifacts. No external network service was contacted.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
1. `OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/feeds/src/cli.test.ts extensions/feeds/src/doctor/register.test.ts extensions/policy/src/doctor/register.test.ts extensions/policy/src/cli.test.ts src/flows/bundled-health-checks.test.ts --reporter=dot`
2. `pnpm exec oxlint --tsconfig ./tsconfig.json extensions/feeds/src/cli.ts extensions/feeds/src/cli.test.ts extensions/feeds/src/feed-document.ts`
3. `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 extensions/feeds/src/cli.ts extensions/feeds/src/cli.test.ts extensions/feeds/src/feed-document.ts extensions/feeds/src/doctor/register.ts extensions/policy/src/doctor/register.ts extensions/policy/src/doctor/register.test.ts extensions/policy/src/policy-state.ts docs/cli/policy.md docs/plugins/reference/feeds.md && git diff --check`
4. `pnpm tsgo:extensions`
5. `node scripts/run-node.mjs feeds validate "file://$proof_root/current.json" --json`
6. `node scripts/run-node.mjs feeds hash "file://$proof_root/current.json"`
7. `node scripts/run-node.mjs feeds build --inventory "$proof_root/inventory.json" --out "$proof_root/built-one.json" --id company-approved --json`
8. `node scripts/run-node.mjs feeds build --inventory "$proof_root/inventory.json" --out "$proof_root/built-two.json" --id company-approved --json`
9. `cmp "$proof_root/built-one.json" "$proof_root/built-two.json"`
10. `node scripts/run-node.mjs feeds diff --previous "$proof_root/previous.json" --current "$proof_root/current.json" --json`
11. `node scripts/run-node.mjs feeds updates --installed "$proof_root/installed.json" --approved-only --json`
12. `node scripts/run-node.mjs feeds notices --installed "$proof_root/installed.json" --approved-only --json`

Evidence after fix:
Validation passed: focused Vitest passed 367 tests across 4 files, oxlint passed with 0 warnings/errors, scoped oxfmt plus `git diff --check` passed, and `pnpm tsgo:extensions` passed.

Source CLI proof transcript:

```text
feeds validate file://.../current.json --json
{
  "ok": true,
  "id": "company-approved",
  "entries": 2,
  "integrity": "sha256:475f7916dc11cdc655e9d5d466a037001b273050942377fb79efe29ef69feeed"
}

feeds hash file://.../current.json
sha256:475f7916dc11cdc655e9d5d466a037001b273050942377fb79efe29ef69feeed

feeds build ... built-one.json
{"out":"/tmp/openclaw-feeds-87827-proof.../built-one.json","integrity":"sha256:d21f9f216d4336b886ef799787e93b4c822f7cecb8e6d06cec45d4ebf2aeed1e","entries":2}

feeds build ... built-two.json
{"out":"/tmp/openclaw-feeds-87827-proof.../built-two.json","integrity":"sha256:d21f9f216d4336b886ef799787e93b4c822f7cecb8e6d06cec45d4ebf2aeed1e","entries":2}

BUILD_ARTIFACTS_IDENTICAL=yes
BUILD_HAS_GENERATED_AT=false
BUILD_ENTRY_ORDER=plugin:calendar-helper,skill:excel-review

feeds diff --previous previous.json --current current.json --json
{"added":[{"kind":"plugin","id":"calendar-helper","version":"0.3.0"}],"removed":[{"kind":"plugin","id":"removed-plugin","version":"0.1.0"}],"updated":[{"kind":"skill","id":"excel-review","from":"1.0.0","to":"1.2.0","hashChanged":true}]}

feeds updates --installed installed.json --approved-only --json
{"updates":[{"kind":"skill","id":"excel-review","installed":"1.0.0","available":"1.2.0","approved":true}]}

feeds notices --installed installed.json --approved-only --json
{"notices":[{"kind":"skill","id":"excel-review","message":"Compliance spreadsheet review skill update is approved.","installCommand":"openclaw skills install /tmp/excel-review"}]}
```

Observed result after fix:
`validate` and `hash` succeeded on `file://` feed URL input through the shared feed byte loader. Two default `build` runs produced byte-identical artifacts with no `generatedAt` field. `diff` reported added, removed, and updated entries with hash-change information. `updates` and `notices` reported the approved `excel-review` update and the expected install command.

What was not tested:
No live external HTTPS endpoint was contacted in the source CLI proof; HTTPS URL loading is covered by the focused runtime-fetch unit regression in `extensions/feeds/src/cli.test.ts`.

## Validation

```bash
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/feeds/src/cli.test.ts extensions/feeds/src/doctor/register.test.ts extensions/policy/src/doctor/register.test.ts extensions/policy/src/cli.test.ts src/flows/bundled-health-checks.test.ts
node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs --noEmit
pnpm exec oxfmt --check extensions/feeds/src/cli.ts extensions/feeds/src/cli.test.ts extensions/feeds/src/feed-document.ts extensions/feeds/src/doctor/register.ts extensions/policy/src/doctor/register.ts extensions/policy/src/doctor/register.test.ts extensions/policy/src/policy-state.ts docs/cli/policy.md docs/plugins/reference/feeds.md docs/plugins/reference.md
git diff --check
node scripts/generate-plugin-inventory-doc.mjs --check
```

Focused stack proof on the final condensed branch: 243 tests passed.

## Feed PR stack

1. openclaw/openclaw#87824 - read-only feed discovery
2. openclaw/openclaw#87825 - approved feed installs
3. openclaw/openclaw#87826 - feed catalog policy conformance
4. openclaw/openclaw#87827 - feed lifecycle tooling
5. openclaw/openclaw#88732 - native feed search defaults and policy checks
6. openclaw/clawhub#2460 - ClawHub root feed lanes

The stack keeps OpenClaw as a feed consumer. ClawHub root feeds are producer infrastructure; enterprise or tenant feeds can be produced elsewhere using the same schema.

RFC draft: https://github.com/giodl73-repo/rfcs/blob/feeds-rfc-draft/rfcs/0004-feeds.md
